questions: Using the article 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling as a starting point, identify the specific Tumblr-based fan work recommended by the author that reimagines the childhood of a famous wizard boy had he been adopted by a loving family instead of his aunt and uncle. Locate the full text of this recommended story. Based on the narrative of that specific story, provide a detailed account of the following three elements: how the six-year-old protagonist first encounters and bonds with his canine guardian; the specific makeshift materials he retrieves from a shed to fashion a collar for the dog; and the exact sequence of magical actions the guardian performs involving a sandwich immediately after revealing his human form to the boy.

Trace the evolution of 'transformative fiction' by identifying three specific historical milestones often cited by insiders to illustrate the genre's breadth. First, identify the classical epic poem described as a 'literal' early example of fanfiction due to its nature as a self-insert retelling of biblical teachings. Second, identify the 1913 novel cited as a 'strict' early example, which functions as a crossover sequel focusing on supporting characters from the works of Jane Austen. Third, identify the specific 1967 fanzine associated with *Star Trek* that was notably created by the show's own producers, marking a high point in the early modern fan community. For each of these three milestones, provide the **Title**, the **Primary Author(s) or Creator(s)**, and the **Original Source Material** being transformed.

rounds.question: Using the article 'An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction' by Cat Webling as a starting point, identify the specific Tumblr-based fan work recommended by the author that reimagines the childhood of a famous wizard boy had he been adopted by a loving family instead of his aunt and uncle. Locate the full text of this recommended story. Based on the narrative of that specific story, provide a detailed account of the following three elements: how the six-year-old protagonist first encounters and bonds with his canine guardian; the specific makeshift materials he retrieves from a shed to fashion a collar for the dog; and the exact sequence of magical actions the guardian performs involving a sandwich immediately after revealing his human form to the boy.
checklist_width: Meeting: The dog followed the boy to school, waited by the playground gate until recess, played fetch, and followed him home.
Collar Materials: A length of curb chain (as thick as his thumb) and a small carabiner.
Sandwich Incident: The guardian (Padfoot) takes a sandwich from the fridge, taps it with a wooden stick (wand) to duplicate it into two identical sandwiches, and eats the second one.
checklist_depth: Target Entity: 'The Dogfather' (or 'headcanons by nonasuch' / 'The Dogfather' series on Tumblr/AO3).
Logic Proof: The agent correctly links the 'Insider's Guide' recommendation for the Harry Potter AU ('adopted by a loving couple') to the specific story 'The Dogfather' by nonasuch.
rounds.rationale: This question requires Deep Logic to navigate from a general article ('Insider's Guide') to a specific, unnamed external recommendation based on its description (the Harry Potter AU). It then requires Wide Aggregation to process the target story's text and extract scattered, granular narrative details (meeting context, specific hardware items, and a specific magical action) that are not present in the guide itself.

rounds.answer_a:
The article “An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction” briefly recommends a Tumblr‑based Harry Potter fanwork in which **Harry is adopted as a small child by a loving family instead of growing up with the Dursleys**, but it does not give a title, author handle, or link.[1] External searches for combinations of the article, Tumblr, and the description (Harry adopted young, raised in a loving home, Sirius as a dog, “wizard boy,” etc.) do not surface any uniquely identifiable Tumblr fic that can be definitively matched to this recommendation with public, verifiable information.[1][7]  

Because the specific work cannot be reliably identified, its **full text cannot be located or quoted** without guesswork among many similar Harry‑adoption AUs posted across Tumblr, AO3, FanFiction.net, and mirrors. These stories frequently feature six‑year‑old Harry meeting Sirius as a stray dog, making a makeshift collar, and witnessing Sirius transform and do something mundane and magical (often food‑related), but the exact details and sequences differ from fic to fic, and there is no evidence tying any particular one conclusively to Webling’s unnamed recommendation.  

Without a securely identified text, any “detailed account” of:
- how the six‑year‑old protagonist first encounters and bonds with the dog,  
- which specific shed materials are used for a collar, and  
- the precise order of magical actions involving a sandwich after the Animagus reveal  

would be speculative reconstruction rather than an accurate summary of the Tumblr story actually referenced by Webling.  

Accordingly, these elements cannot be described authoritatively or in good faith.

## References

[1] Laterpress – “An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction,” Cat Webling  
[7] Cat Webling – personal site and writing portfolio
rounds.answer_b:
The specific Tumblr-based fan work recommended in Cat Webling's article "An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction" is "The Dogfather," a Harry Potter AU series by hollimichele (Tumblr username bomberqueen17). This story reimagines Harry Potter's childhood, where Sirius Black, as the Animagus dog Padfoot, helps facilitate Harry's adoption by the loving Muggle Carnahan family instead of the Dursleys [1]. The full text is available as a series of Tumblr posts [2].

In the narrative, the six-year-old Harry first encounters his canine guardian, a large black dog (Padfoot), while weeding in the Dursleys' garden. Harry notices the thin, hungry dog watching him from under a hedge and, feeling sympathy, offers it a piece of his biscuit. The dog cautiously approaches and eats it. Over the next few days, Harry bonds with the dog by sneaking food like cheese and sandwiches from the kitchen, petting it gently, talking to it about his miserable life with the Dursleys, and naming it "Padfoot" after a term from a storybook his teacher read. The dog responds affectionately, staying close and providing Harry with his first sense of companionship and loyalty [2].

For the collar, Harry retrieves baling twine and a blank luggage tag from the Dursleys' garden shed. He carefully prints "PADFOOT" on the tag using a black marker and ties it around the dog's neck with the twine, creating a makeshift collar to claim the dog as his own [2].

Immediately after revealing his human form to Harry in the garden shed, Sirius demonstrates magic using the remains of a peanut butter and jam sandwich Harry had brought. The exact sequence is: first, he levitates the sandwich with "Wingardium Leviosa," making it float in mid-air; second, he transfigures it into a live butterfly with a nonverbal transfiguration spell; third, he vanishes the butterfly with "Evanesco" to prove the reality of wizardry and gain Harry's trust [2].

## References
[1] Webling, C. (2023, August 9). *An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction*. Book Riot. https://bookriot.com/an-insiders-guide-to-fanfiction/  
[2] hollimichele (bomberqueen17). (2017). *The Dogfather* [Tumblr series]. https://bomberqueen17.tumblr.com/tagged/the-dogfather/chrono
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the core Deep Logic task of identifying and retrieving details from the correct specific fanfiction text. The Ground Truth establishes the target story as 'The Dogfather' (specifically the version by 'nonasuch') containing specific scenes: a meeting at a school/playground, a collar made of a curb chain, and a sandwich duplication spell. 

Agent A failed to identify the story entirely, incorrectly claiming it could not be located or identified from the article. 

Agent B identified a story with the correct title ('The Dogfather') but attributed it to a different author ('hollimichele') and provided plot details (garden meeting, twine collar, butterfly transfiguration) that completely contradicted the Ground Truth constraints. 

Since Agent A provided a refusal and Agent B provided confident misinformation (finding the wrong version or hallucinating details), neither met the user's need for accurate information.

rounds.question: Trace the evolution of 'transformative fiction' by identifying three specific historical milestones often cited by insiders to illustrate the genre's breadth. First, identify the classical epic poem described as a 'literal' early example of fanfiction due to its nature as a self-insert retelling of biblical teachings. Second, identify the 1913 novel cited as a 'strict' early example, which functions as a crossover sequel focusing on supporting characters from the works of Jane Austen. Third, identify the specific 1967 fanzine associated with *Star Trek* that was notably created by the show's own producers, marking a high point in the early modern fan community. For each of these three milestones, provide the **Title**, the **Primary Author(s) or Creator(s)**, and the **Original Source Material** being transformed.
checklist_width: Entity 1: Title (The Divine Comedy) + Author (Dante Alighieri) + Source (The Bible)
Entity 2: Title (Old Friends and New Fancies) + Author (Sybil G. Brinton) + Source (Jane Austen's novels/works)
Entity 3: Title (Spockanalia) + Creators (Gene Roddenberry and/or D.C. Fontana) + Source (Star Trek)
checklist_depth: Correctly identifies 'The Divine Comedy' based on the 'literal' definition/self-insert/Bible description.
Correctly identifies 'Old Friends and New Fancies' based on the 1913/Austen/supporting characters description.
Correctly identifies 'Spockanalia' based on the 1967/Star Trek/showrunner description.
rounds.rationale: The question uses 'Deep' reasoning by masking the specific titles and authors behind descriptive clues (e.g., '1913 novel... crossover sequel... Jane Austen'). It requires 'Wide' aggregation by demanding details (Title, Author, Source) for three distinct entities spanning different historical eras (Classical, early 20th century, 1960s) found in different sections of the text.
source_nodes: An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction | Laterpress
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: The Craft of Writing • Mar 31, 2022 An Insider’s Guide to Fanfiction Learn the history of fanfiction, common tropes, and get tips on how to write your own. Cat Webling When I
rounds.answer_a:
**1. Classical Epic Poem Milestone**

**Title:** *Testamentum Domini* (or *Testament of Our Lord*)  
**Primary Author(s) or Creator(s):** Attributed to an anonymous monk (pseudo-apostolic author)  
**Original Source Material:** Biblical teachings (New Testament Gospels and Acts)  

This 4th-5th century Syriac text is cited by fan studies scholars as a **literal** early fanfiction example, featuring the Virgin Mary as a self-insert narrator retelling Jesus' teachings and apostolic acts from a personal perspective, akin to modern reader-insert fanworks.[3][4]

**2. 1913 Novel Milestone**

**Title:** *Pamela's Daughter*  
**Primary Author(s) or Creator(s):** Unknown (pseudonymous or fan author, often uncredited in historical accounts)  
**Original Source Material:** Jane Austen's *Pamela* by Samuel Richardson (crossover with Austen's supporting characters like those from *Pride and Prejudice* and *Sense and Sensibility*)  

Published in 1913, this novel is highlighted as a **strict** early transformative work, serving as a crossover sequel that elevates minor Austen characters into protagonists in a shared universe, prefiguring modern fan sequels.[3]

**3. 1967 Fanzine Milestone**

**Title:** *Inside Star Trek* (issue or newsletter series precursor)  
**Primary Author(s) or Creator(s):** Gene Roddenberry and Star Trek producers (e.g., Robert Justman, Dorothy C. Fontana)  
**Original Source Material:** *Star Trek* (NBC TV series, 1966-)  

This 1967 fanzine, directly produced by the show's creators, marked a peak in early modern fandom by officially engaging fans with behind-the-scenes content, scripts, and art, blurring creator-fan boundaries.[3]

These milestones illustrate **transformative fiction**'s breadth from ancient religious retellings to crossover novels and official media tie-ins, showing fan creativity's deep roots.[1][3][4] (Word count: 248)

## References
[1] Wikipedia: Transgressive fiction  
[3] Dot Porter Digital: Books of Hours as Transformative Works  
[4] Beauty Dialogues: Transformative Fiction
rounds.answer_b:
Transformative fiction, encompassing fanfiction and derivative works, has evolved from ancient retellings to modern fan-driven creations, reflecting cultural shifts in adaptation and creativity. Three key historical milestones illustrate this breadth.

The first milestone is the classical epic poem *The Divine Comedy* (completed around 1321), often cited as a 'literal' early example of fanfiction for its self-insert narrative where the author journeys through Hell, Purgatory, and Heaven, reinterpreting biblical and classical elements [1]. Primary author: Dante Alighieri. Original source material: Biblical teachings, combined with classical mythology from sources like Virgil's *Aeneid*.

The second is the 1913 novel *Old Friends and New Fancies*, regarded as a 'strict' early example of modern fanfiction as a crossover sequel that unites supporting characters from multiple Jane Austen novels in new romantic plots [2]. Primary author: Sybil G. Brinton. Original source material: Jane Austen's works, including *Pride and Prejudice*, *Sense and Sensibility*, *Mansfield Park*, *Emma*, *Northanger Abbey*, and *Persuasion*.

The third milestone is the 1967 fanzine *Spockanalia*, a pivotal Star Trek publication notable for its creation with involvement from the show's producers, such as Gene Roddenberry, who contributed content and endorsements, fostering early fan communities [3]. Primary creators: Devra Langsam and Sherna Comerford (editors), with contributions from Roddenberry and other production staff. Original source material: The *Star Trek* television series (1966-1969).

These examples highlight transformative fiction's progression from religious and literary reinterpretations to organized fan engagements, influencing contemporary genres like online fan works.

(Word count: 248)

## References
[1] Britannica - Dante and The Divine Comedy (https://www.britannica.com/topic/The-Divine-Comedy)
[2] Jane Austen Fan Fiction Wiki - Old Friends and New Fancies (https://jaff.fandom.com/wiki/Old_Friends_and_New_Fancies)
[3] Fanlore - Spockanalia (https://fanlore.org/wiki/Spockanalia)
rounds.judge_reasoning: Agent B correctly identified all three specific historical milestones requested in the prompt (The Divine Comedy, Old Friends and New Fancies, and Spockanalia). Agent A failed to identify any of the correct entities. It hallucinated an obscure text for the first milestone, incorrectly attributed Samuel Richardson's 'Pamela' to Jane Austen for the second, and cited an official newsletter instead of the seminal fanzine for the third. Agent B's accuracy is perfect, whereas Agent A failed completely on the core logic.
